sources.name: CIA World Factbook — Cuba (current edition, accessed via Theodora.com mirror; cia.gov blocked automated access)
CIA World Factbook — Cuba Economy page (Theodora mirror)
CIA World Factbook — Cuba People & Society / Energy / Communications (Theodora mirror)

population.note: Population is declining due to negative growth and net out-migration (-2.5 migrants/1,000). World Bank estimates ~11.0 million.

gdp.official: $93.79 billion (official exchange rate, at 1 CUP = 1 US$)
gdp.real_growth: 1.25% (2021 est.); -10.95% (2020 est.); -0.16% (2019 est.). World Bank cross-check: approximately -1.1% (2024).
gdp.per_capita: $12,300 (2016 est., in 2016 US dollars). World Bank current-US$ per capita cross-check: ~$9,600 (2024).

labor_force.note: The Factbook no longer reports a labor-force-by-occupation (agriculture/industry/services) breakdown for Cuba. It instead reports the sector split: state sector 72.3%, non-state sector 27.7% (2021 est.). Unemployment rate 2.76% (2021 est.); unofficial estimates roughly double.

exports.note: Data are in current-year dollars and exclude illicit exports or re-exports. Commodities figures are 2021; partner shares are 2019.

energy.generation_mix: fossil fuels 95.5%, biomass and waste 2.7%, solar 1.4%, hydroelectricity 0.3%, wind 0.1%, nuclear 0% (share of total installed capacity, 2020 est.)
energy.note: Consumption 16,097,460,000 kWh (2019 est.); transmission/distribution losses 3.429 billion kWh (2019 est.). Petroleum production 38,400 bbl/day (2021 est.); crude oil reserves ~124 million barrels (2021 est.). Despite 100% nominal electrification, Cuba has faced severe, recurring grid failures and rolling blackouts in recent years not captured by these Factbook figures.

telecom.mobile_subscriptions: 7,103,296 total mobile-cellular subscriptions; 63 per 100 inhabitants (2021 est.)
telecom.note: Factbook structured field reports 71% internet penetration (2021 est.), but its own telecom assessment text states only ~64% of Cubans have internet access and ~60% have cell service. Fixed-line subscriptions 1,573,526 (2022 est., ~14 per 100). Fixed broadband only ~2 per 100 (2020 est.). State controls the telecom sector; access is censored; Cuba has among the lowest mobile/internet rates in the region.

challenges: Still largely state-run, centrally planned economy; private sector only gradually expanding under the new constitution
Chronic shortages of basic necessities (food, medicine, fuel) and severe, recurring electricity blackouts driving public protests
Heavy import dependence: imports (~$11 billion) dwarf exports (~$2.6 billion), producing a large structural trade deficit
Dependence on Venezuela via the oil-for-doctors relationship, exposing Cuba to a key partner's instability
US economic embargo constraining trade, finance, and investment
High public debt and budget deficit (deficit ~10.8% of GDP, 2017 est.); weak external reserves and external debt ~$30 billion
Dual/volatile currency legacy and distorted official exchange rate; high inflation pressures
Declining and aging population (negative growth, high out-migration, elderly dependency ratio rising) shrinking the labor force
Low telecom/internet penetration and state censorship hindering the digital economy
notes: PRIMARY DATA STALENESS CAVEAT: The CIA World Factbook's core macroeconomic figures for Cuba are notably stale — GDP (PPP and official), GDP composition by sector, exports/imports values, and fiscal data are all 2017 estimates (PPP per capita is 2016). The most recent figures are real GDP growth and labor force (2021 est.) and population (2023 est.). Cuba publishes limited and unreliable official statistics, so even authoritative cross-checks diverge: World Bank current-US$ figures imply GDP ~$107 billion and per-capita ~$9,600 with negative growth (~-1.1%) in 2024, which is not directly comparable to the Factbook's 2016-dollar PPP basis. Where the Factbook field value and its own narrative disagree (internet penetration 71% vs ~64%), both are noted. Data accessed via the Theodora.com mirror of the current Factbook because cia.gov blocked automated retrieval (HTTP 403); figures match the current CIA World Factbook edition. Fields the Factbook does not provide for Cuba (labor force by occupation) are set to null.
